text: In this video, we present the main steps to perform a pure robotic left hemipatectomy. In this picture, the relative position of the patient, the surgeons and the surgical robot is represented. The extra-glycinean approach is preferred for anatomic hepatectomy. As in laparoscopic surgery, in robotic surgery the Pringle maneuver is mandatory and must be prepared to prevent and manage bleeding problems during the course of surgery. The use of the Wong loop is very useful and very easy to do with robotic devices. To do this, we use a urinary catheter.

The surgeon divides the round ligament of the liver, followed by the suspensory ligament, progressing towards the hepatic veins. The left triangular ligament is divided, and the right triangular ligament is released to expose the edge of the right hepatic vein. The surgeon prepares the extra-glycinean approach to the left hilar pedicle by elevating the lower face of the liver and opening the lesser omentum until reaching the aranchium ligament. Blunt maneuvers are used to detach the Lien X capsule, following Takasaki and Sujika's concepts, and Gate III is identified.

The surgeon isolates the left hilar pedicle and temporarily clamps it, creating a visible ischemic zone. Indocyanine green is used to clearly demarcate the line of ischemia for the anatomic hepatectomy.

The surgical robot displays a coupled image of the intraoperative ultrasound, aiding in the identification of the middle and left hepatic veins. The tumor is located near the outlet of the middle hepatic vein. The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are used for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. As the partition progresses, polymer clips are applied, and veins in segments IVB and IVA are identified for cleaning, sealing, and sectioning.

The surgeon uses a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line to section the left hilar pedicle, adjusting the staple size according to tissue thickness. Three-point-five millimeter staples are used for the pedicle, while two-point-five millimeter staples are used for the left hepatic vein. The surgical specimen is placed in a plastic bag, and hemostasis of the parenchyma and staple section lines is checked. Indocyanine green is used to verify the absence of ischemic areas.
